questions.text: The derivative of a constant is…
questions.explanation: A constant function has a horizontal graph, so its gradient — and hence its derivative — is $0$ everywhere.

questions.text: Differentiating $f(x) = x^2$ from first principles gives $\dfrac{(x+h)^2 - x^2}{h} = 2x + h$. What is $f'(x)$?
questions.explanation: Taking the limit as $h \to 0$ removes the $h$ term, leaving $f'(x) = 2x$.

questions.text: Which of the following functions have derivative equal to $6x$?
questions.explanation: $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(3x^2) = 6x$ and $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(3x^2 + 5) = 6x$ (the constant $5$ differentiates to $0$). By contrast $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(x^2) = 2x$ and $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(6x) = 6$.

questions.text: Find the gradient of the curve $y = x^2 - 4x + 3$ at the point where $x = 3$.
Name the rule used to differentiate a term of the form $ax^n$.
questions.explanation: Differentiating $ax^n$ to get $anx^{n-1}$ is the **power rule**: bring the power down and reduce it by one.
$7x = 7x^1$, so by the power rule $f'(x) = 7 \times 1 \times x^{0} = 7$. The gradient of the line $y = 7x$ is $7$ everywhere.

questions.text: Using the power rule, $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(a x^n)$ equals…
questions.explanation: The power rule states $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}x}(ax^n) = anx^{n-1}$: multiply by the power and reduce the power by one.
